title: Common Query Pitfalls & Anti-patterns

glossary.definition: A period when a system or service becomes unavailable, often caused by severe performance issues or crashes.
Commonly used solutions or coding practices that actually create more problems than they solve.
A mathematical term describing a result where every single item in one list is paired with every single item in another list.

overview.what: Query pitfalls are common mistakes that return incorrect row-sets or cause severe performance issues. Examples include accidental cross-joins, using SELECT * unnecessarily, or applying functions on indexed columns which forces full table scans.
overview.why: Writing SQL that 'works' on small datasets often masks underlying anti-patterns. Knowing these pitfalls prevents production outages when data scales.

why_learning_this: Avoid performance cliffs caused by hidden table scans.
Ensure your aggregations and joins return accurate row counts.

common_mistakes.title: Missing JOIN conditions
common_mistakes.explanation: Forgetting an ON clause or WHERE condition in a join creates a Cartesian product (cross join), returning every combination of rows. Fix: Always explicitly define how tables relate.

recall_questions.q: Why is it an anti-pattern to use `SELECT *` in application code?
recall_questions.answer: It fetches unnecessary data, wasting network and memory, and can break applications if the underlying table schema changes.

understanding_checks.question: What rows does this return?
understanding_checks.why: Counts all 8 customers. A common pitfall is counting a column that has NULLs when you mean to count all rows (like COUNT(*)).

understanding_checks.question: What rows does this return?
understanding_checks.why: Customer 5 (Emma) has a NULL country. COUNT(column) ignores NULL values, which is a frequent source of accidental bugs.

query_walkthrough.query: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders) ORDER BY id;
query_walkthrough.focus: Using IN with a subquery is valid, but can sometimes perform poorly compared to EXISTS or a JOIN on larger datasets. Here it filters to the 6 customers who have orders.

practice_tasks.title: Fixing the COUNT pitfall
practice_tasks.prompt: Given this query that accidentally ignores NULL countries, change it to safely count all customer rows regardless of NULLs.

challenge.title: Avoid SELECT *
challenge.prompt: Write a query that joins products and order_items for order_id 1, but instead of using SELECT *, explicitly select the product name and the quantity ordered.
challenge.solution: SELECT p.name, oi.quantity FROM products p JOIN order_items oi ON p.id = oi.product_id WHERE oi.order_id = 1 ORDER BY p.id;
